name: Java
description: Master Java from fundamentals to advanced design patterns.

contentBlocks.content: Java is a popular programming language, created in 1995. It is owned by Oracle, and more than 3 billion devices run Java.

It is used for:
• Mobile applications (specially Android apps)
• Desktop applications
• Web applications
• Web servers and application servers
• Games
• Database connection
• And much, much more!

Java has several distinctive features that make it a powerful language:

• Simple & Easy to Learn: Java syntax is clean and straightforward
• Object-Oriented: Everything is an object with attributes and methods
• Platform Independent: "Write Once, Run Anywhere" (WORA)
• Secure: Built-in security features and exception handling
• Robust: Strong memory management and error handling
• Multi-threaded: Supports concurrent programming
• High Performance: Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation

To start programming in Java, you need to:

1. Install JDK (Java Development Kit) from oracle.com
2. Set up JAVA_HOME environment variable
3. Verify installation: java -version
4. Use an IDE like IntelliJ IDEA, Eclipse, or VS Code
5. Write your first program and compile it with: javac filename.java
6. Run it with: java filename

# Check Java version
java -version

# Compile Java file
javac HelloWorld.java

# Run compiled program
java HelloWorld
Variables are containers for storing data values. Java is a statically-typed language, meaning you must declare the type of data a variable will hold.

Primitive Data Types:
• byte: 8-bit signed integer (-128 to 127)
• short: 16-bit signed integer
• int: 32-bit signed integer
• long: 64-bit signed integer
• float: 32-bit floating point
• double: 64-bit floating point
• boolean: true or false
• char: single character

Operators are used to perform operations on variables and values:

• Arithmetic Operators: +, -, *, /, %, ++, --
• Assignment Operators: =, +=, -=, *=, /=
• Comparison Operators: ==, !=, >, <, >=, <=
• Logical Operators: &&, ||, !
• Bitwise Operators: &, |, ^, ~, <<, >>

Control flow statements determine the flow of program execution:

• if-else: Execute code based on conditions
• switch: Execute one block among many
• for loop: Repeat code a specific number of times
• while loop: Repeat code while condition is true
• do-while loop: Execute at least once, then repeat while true
Java is an object-oriented programming language. Everything in Java is associated with classes and objects, along with its attributes and methods. A class is a blueprint for creating objects.

Class Components:
• Attributes (properties/fields): Variables that belong to the object
• Methods: Functions that define the behavior of the object
• Constructor: Special method used to initialize objects
• Modifiers: Control access level (public, private, protected)

Inheritance allows a class to inherit attributes and methods from another class. It promotes code reuse and establishes a relationship between classes. The class that inherits is called a subclass (child), and the class being inherited from is called a superclass (parent).

Key Concepts:
• Superclass: The parent class being inherited from
• Subclass: The child class inheriting from the superclass
• extends keyword: Used to inherit from a class
• super keyword: Refers to the parent class
• Method Overriding: Child class provides new implementation of parent method

Polymorphism means "many forms". It allows you to perform a single action in many different ways. There are two types of polymorphism in Java:

• Compile-time (Static) Polymorphism: Method Overloading
• Runtime (Dynamic) Polymorphism: Method Overriding

Method Overloading: Same method name, different parameters (number, type, or order)
Method Overriding: Child class overrides parent class method with same signature
Encapsulation is the bundling of data (variables) and methods (functions) into a single unit called a class. It hides the internal details and provides controlled access through public methods.

Benefits:
• Data Hiding: Keep internal state private
• Controlled Access: Use getters and setters to control access
• Flexibility: Modify internal implementation without affecting external code
• Validation: Validate data before setting values
Abstraction is the process of hiding complex implementation details and showing only the necessary features. It helps reduce complexity and increases efficiency.

In Java, abstraction is achieved through:
• Abstract Classes: Cannot be instantiated, can have abstract methods
• Interfaces: Define contracts that classes must implement
• Abstract Methods: Declared without implementation, subclasses must implement them
An interface is a reference type in Java. It is similar to a class but it only contains constants and abstract methods. Interfaces are used to achieve abstraction and multiple inheritance.

Interface Features:
• All methods are abstract by default
• All variables are static and final
• Cannot have constructors
• A class can implement multiple interfaces
• One interface can extend another interface

Exception handling is a mechanism to handle runtime errors, enabling a program to continue execution even after an error occurs. Exceptions are events that disrupt the normal flow of program execution.

Key Components:
• try: Block containing code that might throw an exception
• catch: Catches and handles the exception
• finally: Block that always executes, regardless of exception
• throw: Explicitly throw an exception
• throws: Declare that method might throw an exception
Collections are containers that hold groups of objects. The Collections Framework provides interfaces and classes for storing, retrieving, and manipulating groups of data efficiently.

Main Collection Types:
• List: Ordered collection, allows duplicates (ArrayList, LinkedList)
• Set: Unordered collection, no duplicates (HashSet, TreeSet)
• Map: Key-value pairs (HashMap, TreeMap)
• Queue: FIFO data structure (PriorityQueue, Deque)
The Streams API (introduced in Java 8) provides a functional programming approach to process sequences of elements. Streams allow for declarative, expressive, and efficient data processing.

Stream Concepts:
• Lazy Evaluation: Operations are performed only when needed
• Functional Style: Uses lambda expressions and method references
• Pipeline: Chain multiple operations (filter, map, reduce)
• Non-interference: Streams don't modify source

Generics enable types (classes and methods) to be parameters when defining classes, interfaces, and methods. They provide a way to write reusable code that works with different data types while maintaining type safety.

Benefits of Generics:
• Type Safety: Catch errors at compile-time instead of runtime
• Code Reusability: Write once, use with any type
• Elimination of Casts: No need for explicit type casting
• Better Performance: Reduced overhead from type checking

Lambda expressions are anonymous functions that enable you to treat functionality as a method argument or return a function as a result. They simplify the syntax for implementing functional interfaces.

Lambda Syntax: (parameters) -> { body }

Features:
• Concise syntax for anonymous functions
• Works with functional interfaces
• Enables functional programming style
• Reduces code verbosity compared to anonymous classes
The Singleton pattern ensures that a class has only one instance and provides a global point of access to it. This is useful for coordinating actions across a system.
The Factory pattern provides an interface for creating objects without specifying the exact classes to create. It promotes loose coupling and easier maintenance.

The Observer pattern defines a one-to-many relationship where when one object changes state, all its dependents are notified automatically. It's useful for implementing distributed event-handling systems.
A thread is a lightweight process that can run independently within a program. Multithreading enables concurrent execution of multiple tasks, improving performance and responsiveness.

Creating Threads:
1. Extend Thread class and override run() method
2. Implement Runnable interface and pass to Thread constructor

The Runnable approach is preferred as Java doesn't support multiple inheritance.

topics.name: Java 8 — Key Additions & Deep Dive
topics.description: In-depth coverage of Java 8 features: Lambdas, Streams, Optional, new Date/Time API, CompletableFuture, collectors, and practical examples.

contentBlocks.content: Java 8 introduced major language and library enhancements focused on functional-style programming and improved APIs. The most notable features are lambda expressions, the Streams API, the Optional type, the java.time date/time API, default and static methods in interfaces, method references, and CompletableFuture for async programming.
This section provides conceptual context and the motivation behind each addition: conciseness, easier parallelism, safer null handling, and clearer date/time handling.

Lambda expressions let you treat functionality as data: pass behavior to methods. A lambda is an anonymous function with a concise syntax: (parameters) -> expression or (parameters) -> { statements }.

Functional interfaces are single-abstract-method interfaces (SAM). Java 8 adds @FunctionalInterface annotation to express intent (optional but useful). The java.util.function package provides common functional interfaces: Function, Consumer, Supplier, Predicate, BiFunction, etc.
Explanation: Lambdas reduce boilerplate, make callbacks concise, and pair naturally with Streams. Choose the right functional interface from java.util.function or create a custom SAM when needed.

Method references are compact lambdas that refer to existing methods. They come in four forms: reference to static method (ContainingClass::staticMethod), reference to instance method of particular object (instance::method), reference to instance method of an arbitrary object of a particular type (ClassName::instanceMethod), and constructor references (ClassName::new).
Explanation: method references improve readability when a lambda only calls an existing method.

Interfaces can now have default (instance) methods with implementation and static methods. This enables API evolution without breaking existing implementations and allows providing helper utilities on the interface type.
Explanation: Use default methods sparingly to evolve interfaces. Resolve conflicts explicitly when multiple interfaces provide the same default method.

Streams provide a high-level, declarative API for processing collections of data. A stream pipeline has a source, zero or more intermediate operations (lazy), and a terminal operation (eager). Streams can be sequential or parallel.
Common intermediate operations: map, filter, flatMap, distinct, sorted, peek, limit, skip. Terminal operations: forEach, collect, reduce, count, findFirst, anyMatch, allMatch, noneMatch, toArray.

Explanation: map transforms elements, flatMap flattens nested structures (e.g., List<List<T>> → Stream<T>), and Collectors provides useful collectors: toList, toSet, toMap, groupingBy, partitioningBy, summarizingInt, joining, and more.
Collectors encapsulate mutable reduction strategies. You can combine collectors with downstream collectors for complex aggregations and use Collector.of to create custom collectors when needed.
Explanation: prefer built-in collectors for maintainability; use custom collectors for performance-critical or specialized cases.

Parallel streams can speed up CPU-bound pipelines by using the common ForkJoinPool. Use parallel streams when the dataset is large, operations are stateless and associative, and thread-safety is ensured for mutable collectors.
Caveats: avoid shared mutable state, be mindful of short pipelines or I/O bound steps (overhead may outweigh benefits), and watch out for thread pool interference with other async tasks.

Optional<T> models presence/absence of value and helps avoid null checks and NullPointerException. Use Optional as a return type for potentially missing values, but avoid using Optional for fields or method parameters in performance-sensitive contexts.
Explanation: prefer map/flatMap/filter to keep code functional and concise; use orElseGet for lazy defaults and orElseThrow when absence should be exceptional.

The java.time API replaces legacy Date/Calendar with immutable, fluent types: LocalDate, LocalTime, LocalDateTime, ZonedDateTime, Instant, Duration, Period, and DateTimeFormatter. It provides better clarity, timezone handling, and thread-safety.
Explanation: use Instant for timestamps, ZonedDateTime for timezone-aware operations, and prefer java.time over legacy APIs. DateTimeFormatter is thread-safe and immutable.

CompletableFuture supports building asynchronous, non-blocking pipelines with functional callbacks. It integrates well with Streams and provides methods like supplyAsync, thenApply, thenCompose, thenAccept, exceptionally, and handle.
Explanation: prefer thenCompose to flatten dependent async calls and thenCombine to combine independent results. Be mindful of thread pools and handle exceptions explicitly to avoid silent failures.
This section shows practical recipes combining features: streaming & grouping data, using Optional to avoid null checks, mapping date/time, and running tasks in parallel with CompletableFuture.

Explanation: combine groupingBy with downstream collectors for multi-step aggregation. Use CompletableFuture for non-blocking orchestration of dependent services. Favor immutability and stateless functions in stream pipelines.

Benefits: concise code, clearer data pipelines, safer null handling, modern date/time handling, and better async APIs.
Trade-offs: lambdas can be harder to debug (stack traces), overuse of parallel streams can cause contention, Optional misuse can cause overhead, and default methods can introduce diamond problems if not designed carefully.

Best practices: favor pure functions in streams, avoid side-effects, use built-in collectors, handle exceptions explicitly in async pipelines, and choose the right abstraction (Optional vs nullable, Stream vs loop).
